APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses knowledge base completion via an embedding model (ITransF) with a novel sparse attention mechanism evaluated on standard KBC benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) using canonical metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10) and many established baselines, which squarely places it within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. The audience of researchers working on representation learning and KBC will find the problem formulation, methodological approach, and evaluation standards appropriate. The comprehensive baseline comparisons and standard datasets indicate correct positioning in the community, making it a strong fit for a top venue. | B(5): The paper addresses knowledge base completion via a novel, interpretable embedding model (ITransF) which fits squarely within top NLP/ML conference topics. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) using standard metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10) and compares to a comprehensive set of baselines, demonstrating improvements and thus speaking to the right experimental standards and audience. The focus on sparse attention to discover shared relation-concepts and claims of interpretability are well positioned for current interests in transferable and explainable representation learning, making it a strong fit for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses knowledge base completion via an embedding model (ITransF) with a novel sparse attention mechanism evaluated on standard KBC benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) using canonical metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10) and many established baselines, which squarely places it within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. The audience of researchers working on representation learning and KBC will find the problem formulation, methodological approach, and evaluation standards appropriate. The comprehensive baseline comparisons and standard datasets indicate correct positioning in the community, making it a strong fit for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses knowledge base completion via a novel, interpretable embedding model (ITransF) which fits squarely within top NLP/ML conference topics. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) using standard metrics (Mean Rank, Hits@10) and compares to a comprehensive set of baselines, demonstrating improvements and thus speaking to the right experimental standards and audience. The focus on sparse attention to discover shared relation-concepts and claims of interpretability are well positioned for current interests in transferable and explainable representation learning, making it a strong fit for the venue.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles the important problem of knowledge base completion and proposes a pragmatic embedding model (ITransF) that uses sparse attention to share projection matrices/concepts, yielding interpretable relation-concept associations and measurable gains on WN18 and FB15k versus many baselines. From an empirical-methods perspective the contribution is useful — it demonstrates that concept-sharing via sparse attention can transfer statistical strength — but based on the summary alone it appears incremental rather than transformative: improvements are reported on standard benchmarks but no code or stated limitations are mentioned, and adoption will hinge on reproducibility, detailed ablations, and scalability analyses. Thus I assess moderate impact: likely to be built on by researchers in KBC but not to radically change the field immediately. | B(4): The paper tackles an important and active problem in KBC—sharing statistical strength across relations—by introducing a sparse attention mechanism that yields interpretable relation-to-concept associations and demonstrably improves mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k versus many strong baselines. The emphasis on sparsity and interpretability differentiates it conceptually from prior factorization and multi-task approaches and is likely to change how researchers think about designing transferable, human-understandable relation models. While evaluation is limited to standard benchmarks, the method’s clear mechanism for transfer and interpretability opens promising follow-on work and practical adoption in embedding-based KB pipelines.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of knowledge base completion and proposes a pragmatic embedding model (ITransF) that uses sparse attention to share projection matrices/concepts, yielding interpretable relation-concept associations and measurable gains on WN18 and FB15k versus many baselines. From an empirical-methods perspective the contribution is useful — it demonstrates that concept-sharing via sparse attention can transfer statistical strength — but based on the summary alone it appears incremental rather than transformative: improvements are reported on standard benchmarks but no code or stated limitations are mentioned, and adoption will hinge on reproducibility, detailed ablations, and scalability analyses. Thus I assess moderate impact: likely to be built on by researchers in KBC but not to radically change the field immediately.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important and active problem in KBC—sharing statistical strength across relations—by introducing a sparse attention mechanism that yields interpretable relation-to-concept associations and demonstrably improves mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k versus many strong baselines. The emphasis on sparsity and interpretability differentiates it conceptually from prior factorization and multi-task approaches and is likely to change how researchers think about designing transferable, human-understandable relation models. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper clearly states its main claim (ITransF with sparse attention transfers strength across relations) and reports empirical improvements on WN18 and FB15k against many baselines, with hyperparameters reported. However, based on the provided summary I see missing technical evidence needed for a solid empirical claim: no mention of ablation studies isolating the sparse-attention or block-iterative components, no multiple-run variance or statistical tests, and no runtime/memory or training-stability analysis to support scalability. These gaps leave some uncertainty about which components drive the gains and about reproducibility (code not mentioned), so while results are promising they are not yet fully supported. | B(4): The paper's main claims—that ITransF uses sparse attention to discover shared relation concepts and improves mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k—are clearly stated and supported by empirical comparisons to a wide set of baselines. The methodology appears sound in model design and baseline selection, and there are no apparent mathematical red flags in the summary. However, the summary omits statistical significance reporting, code release, and any discussion of limitations or variance, leaving some concerns about reproducibility and the robustness of the reported gains. Overall the technical contribution seems correct with minor issues that do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (ITransF with sparse attention transfers strength across relations) and reports empirical improvements on WN18 and FB15k against many baselines, with hyperparameters reported. However, based on the provided summary I see missing technical evidence needed for a solid empirical claim: no mention of ablation studies isolating the sparse-attention or block-iterative components, no multiple-run variance or statistical tests, and no runtime/memory or training-stability analysis to support scalability. These gaps leave some uncertainty about which components drive the gains and about reproducibility (code not mentioned), so while results are promising they are not yet fully supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims—that ITransF uses sparse attention to discover shared relation concepts and improves mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k—are clearly stated and supported by empirical comparisons to a wide set of baselines. The methodology appears sound in model design and baseline selection, and there are no apparent mathematical red flags in the summary. However, the summary omits statistical significance reporting, code release, and any discussion of limitations or variance, leaving some concerns about reproducibility and the robustness of the reported gains. Overall the technical contribution seems correct with minor issues that do not undermine the main conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates ITransF on standard WN18 and FB15k and reports improvements on mean rank and Hits@10 with hyperparameters given, so empirical evidence is present but limited to two benchmarks and lacks runtime/memory or scaling measures. There is no mention of ablation studies isolating the sparse-attention or block-iterative optimization contributions, nor are failure modes or limitations discussed; reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but no code, seeds, or pseudocode). Related work coverage appears thorough, but overall the experimental validation is adequate yet misses key analyses (ablation, variance, computational cost) needed to firmly support the claims. | B(3): The experiments compare ITransF against a wide set of baselines on standard WN18 and FB15k benchmarks and report meaningful metric gains, which supports the basic performance claim. However the paper summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of sparsity, number of concepts, or attention sparsity constraints), nor any qualitative evaluation validating the claimed interpretability of learned concepts. Related work is described as thorough, but the paper omits discussion of limitations and does not indicate released code — reproduction is partially supported by reported hyperparameters and optimization details but would be helped by more experimental analyses and artifacts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates ITransF on standard WN18 and FB15k and reports improvements on mean rank and Hits@10 with hyperparameters given, so empirical evidence is present but limited to two benchmarks and lacks runtime/memory or scaling measures. There is no mention of ablation studies isolating the sparse-attention or block-iterative optimization contributions, nor are failure modes or limitations discussed; reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but no code, seeds, or pseudocode). Related work coverage appears thorough, but overall the experimental validation is adequate yet misses key analyses (ablation, variance, computational cost) needed to firmly support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare ITransF against a wide set of baselines on standard WN18 and FB15k benchmarks and report meaningful metric gains, which supports the basic performance claim. However the paper summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of sparsity, number of concepts, or attention sparsity constraints), nor any qualitative evaluation validating the claimed interpretability of learned concepts. Related work is described as thorough, but the paper omits discussion of limitations and does not indicate released code — reproduction is partially supported by reported hyperparameters and optimization details but would be helped by more experimental analyses and artifacts.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a well-studied KB completion problem and mainly builds on the large family of translation- and projection-based embedding models (e.g., TransE/TransR/TransD, STransE) while introducing sparse attention over shared projection matrices to transfer strength across relations. This sparse-concept/attention formulation is a non-trivial and interpretable extension of existing embedding architectures, but it does not appear to introduce a fundamentally new problem formulation or a radically different modeling paradigm relative to prior KBC work. Given the extensive prior literature on relation transfer and projection-based models, the contribution reads as a moderate, incremental novelty that would be informative but not surprising to experts. | B(3): The paper offers a modestly novel combination: using sparse attention over shared projection matrices to induce interpretable ‘concepts’ for relation transfer in KBC. However, the core problem (link prediction) and the idea of sharing components across relations are well-studied (e.g., relation factorization, multi-task sharing and component-based relation models), so the contribution reads as an incremental, non-trivial extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The interpretability and sparse attention framing is nice and distinguishes it from many baselines, but the summary and retrieved literature suggest the technical leap beyond prior transfer/factorization approaches is limited.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied KB completion problem and mainly builds on the large family of translation- and projection-based embedding models (e.g., TransE/TransR/TransD, STransE) while introducing sparse attention over shared projection matrices to transfer strength across relations. This sparse-concept/attention formulation is a non-trivial and interpretable extension of existing embedding architectures, but it does not appear to introduce a fundamentally new problem formulation or a radically different modeling paradigm relative to prior KBC work. Given the extensive prior literature on relation transfer and projection-based models, the contribution reads as a moderate, incremental novelty that would be informative but not surprising to experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a modestly novel combination: using sparse attention over shared projection matrices to induce interpretable ‘concepts’ for relation transfer in KBC. However, the core problem (link prediction) and the idea of sharing components across relations are well-studied (e.g., relation factorization, multi-task sharing and component-based relation models), so the contribution reads as an incremental, non-trivial extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The interpretability and sparse attention framing is nice and distinguishes it from many baselines, but the summary and retrieved literature suggest the technical leap beyond prior transfer/factorization approaches is limited.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method to experiments, with the main contributions (ITransF and sparse attention for transferable concepts) stated early. Notation and definitions appear consistent and supported by reported hyperparameters, and figures/tables (e.g., results on WN18 and FB15k) effectively communicate improvements over many baselines. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though a few sections (e.g., the block iterative optimization details) could be expanded for greater clarity and reproducibility. Minor polish on algorithm pseudocode and an explicit link to code would make the presentation exceptional. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation to method (ITransF) and experiments on WN18/FB15k, and the main contributions (sparse attention over shared projection matrices and interpretability) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented coherently enough to understand the model and training (including block iterative optimization), and hyperparameters and related work are thoroughly reported. The writing quality is described as good and examples of evaluation (mean rank, Hits@10) are provided, though the summary does not detail figures/tables so a few presentation elements may have minor room for improvement.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method to experiments, with the main contributions (ITransF and sparse attention for transferable concepts) stated early. Notation and definitions appear consistent and supported by reported hyperparameters, and figures/tables (e.g., results on WN18 and FB15k) effectively communicate improvements over many baselines. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though a few sections (e.g., the block iterative optimization details) could be expanded for greater clarity and reproducibility. Minor polish on algorithm pseudocode and an explicit link to code would make the presentation exceptional.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation to method (ITransF) and experiments on WN18/FB15k, and the main contributions (sparse attention over shared projection matrices and interpretability) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented coherently enough to understand the model and training (including block iterative optimization), and hyperparameters and related work are thoroughly reported. The writing quality is described as good and examples of evaluation (mean rank, Hits@10) are provided, though the summary does not detail figures/tables so a few presentation elements may have minor room for improvement.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a broad and relevant set of baselines (TransE/TransH/TransR/TransD/CTransR/TransD/SG/NTN/DISTMULT/STransE/KG2E/PTransE, random walk and IRN) on standard benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) using canonical metrics (mean rank and Hits@10), which constitutes a strong empirical comparison. Related work coverage is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility of comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate whether the authors report statistical variance, explicitly acknowledge when some prior methods are comparable/better, or include every recent strong competitor (e.g., tensor-factorization variants like ComplEx are present in the retrieval list but not clearly compared in tables), so I mark it as very good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against a large and appropriate set of classical and contemporary baselines (TransE/TransH/TransR/TransD/CTransR/STransE/DistMult/NTN/TATEC/KG2E/PTransE and others) on standard benchmarks (WN18 and FB15k) using standard metrics (Mean Rank and Hits@10), which supports fair empirical comparison. The authors explicitly note improvements over baselines that do not use additional information and the related-work coverage is reported as thorough. However, given the rapid evolution of KBC methods around 2017, the paper appears to omit some contemporaneous factorization/complex-tensor approaches and recent baseline re-evaluations (e.g., ComplEx and the “baselines strike back” critiques), so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a broad and relevant set of baselines (TransE/TransH/TransR/TransD/CTransR/TransD/SG/NTN/DISTMULT/STransE/KG2E/PTransE, random walk and IRN) on standard benchmarks (WN18, FB15k) using canonical metrics (mean rank and Hits@10), which constitutes a strong empirical comparison. Related work coverage is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility of comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate whether the authors report statistical variance, explicitly acknowledge when some prior methods are comparable/better, or include every recent strong competitor (e.g., tensor-factorization variants like ComplEx are present in the retrieval list but not clearly compared in tables), so I mark it as very good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a large and appropriate set of classical and contemporary baselines (TransE/TransH/TransR/TransD/CTransR/STransE/DistMult/NTN/TATEC/KG2E/PTransE and others) on standard benchmarks (WN18 and FB15k) using standard metrics (Mean Rank and Hits@10), which supports fair empirical comparison. The authors explicitly note improvements over baselines that do not use additional information and the related-work coverage is reported as thorough. However, given the rapid evolution of KBC methods around 2017, the paper appears to omit some contemporaneous factorization/complex-tensor approaches and recent baseline re-evaluations (e.g., ComplEx and the “baselines strike back” critiques), so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: Proposes a novel embedding model ITransF for knowledge base completion.
ITransF uses a sparse attention mechanism to discover hidden concepts of relations and transfer statistical strength through sharing of concepts.
The learned associations between relations and concepts are represented by sparse attention vectors that are easily interpretable.
ITransF obtains improvements on mean rank and Hits@10 on WN18 and FB15k over baselines that do not use additional information.
_summary.methodology: ITransF is an embedding model that employs a sparse attention mechanism to represent relations as combinations of shared projection matrices/concepts, enabling transfer of statistical strength across relations; models are trained with minibatch SGD and a block iterative optimization inspired by LightRNN.

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in machine learning and several years building and reproducing embedding models for large-scale knowledge bases and recommendation systems.
traits: insists on thorough empirical validation: clear baselines, hyperparameter settings, and multiple runs with variance reported
looks for ablation studies that isolate contributions of novel components (e.g., sparse attention, block iterative optimization)
evaluates training stability, computational cost, and scalability (wall-clock times, memory, complexity)
checks reproducibility: requests pseudocode, dataset/preprocessing details, and releaseable code or seed/initialization details
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and reproducibility, asking for targeted experiments and clear comparisons to establish which parts of the method drive improvements.

reviewer_b.name: Knowledge Base Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Professor in natural language processing and knowledge representation who has published multiple papers on knowledge base completion and relational embedding models.
traits: assesses novelty and how the approach compares conceptually to prior transfer or factorization methods (e.g., relation clustering, multi-task sharing, attention over relation components)
values interpretability claims and will probe whether the learned 'concepts' meaningfully correspond to linguistic or ontological structures
considers broader impact and applicability: whether the method generalizes beyond standard benchmarks and its implications for downstream KB tasks
notices connections to contemporary literature and expects clear positioning with respect to state-of-the-art methods and their assumptions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's conceptual contribution and significance within the KBC literature, emphasizing clarity in claims about novelty, interpretability, and potential impact.
